lessons.title: Living in Kilobytes: Measuring Memory & Avoiding the Heap
lessons.content:
**Hook.** On a laptop you almost never worry about memory — you have gigabytes. A *microcontroller* (the tiny computer-on-a-chip inside a thermostat, a toy, or a smart bulb) has only **kilobytes**. A kilobyte is about a thousand bytes; a gigabyte is about a billion. So a microcontroller has roughly a *million times less* room. When space is that tight, you must be frugal and count every byte.

**Build.** A microcontroller has two separate kinds of memory, and they are two separate budgets:

- **Flash** — where your *program* is stored. It keeps its contents even when the power is off, like a tiny built-in hard drive. Your code and any fixed constants live here.
- **RAM** — *working memory* the program scribbles in while it runs. It is wiped when power goes off. Variables, the *stack* (the scratchpad for function calls), and any *heap* live here.

You do not have to guess how much you use. The build tools print a **size report**: a few numbers telling you how many bytes of flash and how many bytes of RAM your program needs. The rule is simple: **measure first, then cut the biggest thing.**

The single most useful habit for a beginner is: **avoid the heap.** The *heap* is memory you ask for while the program is running and give back later ("grow-on-demand" memory). On a big computer that is fine. On a chip with kilobytes it is risky: requests can fail unpredictably after the device has been running a while, and that is hard to recover from in the field. Instead, use **static buffers** — fixed-size storage you decide on ahead of time, before the program runs. You always know exactly how much it costs.

**Recap.** Microcontrollers live in kilobytes, with flash for code and RAM for working data. Measure usage with the size report, cut the biggest hog, and prefer fixed-size static buffers over grow-on-demand heap memory.

items.prompt: Use the diagram. It shows two separate memory budgets on a microcontroller. Which pairing is correct?
choices: Flash stores the program and keeps it when power is off; RAM is working memory wiped at power-off
Flash is wiped at power-off; RAM keeps the program forever
Flash and RAM are the same memory with two names
Flash holds variables while running; RAM holds the program
items.explanation: Flash is non-volatile storage for code and constants — it survives power-off, like a tiny built-in drive. RAM is volatile working memory for variables and the stack, and is wiped when power is removed. They are two distinct budgets, not the same memory.

items.prompt: Roughly how much memory does a typical microcontroller have, compared to a laptop?
choices: About the same as a laptop
Kilobytes — dramatically less than a laptop's gigabytes
More than a laptop, because it is dedicated to one job
Memory does not exist on a microcontroller
items.explanation: A microcontroller typically has kilobytes (thousands of bytes), while a laptop has gigabytes (billions). That is roughly a million times less, which is exactly why you must budget memory carefully on a microcontroller.

items.prompt: Where is your program kept so that it is still there after you unplug the device and plug it back in?
choices: In RAM
In flash
On the stack
It is re-downloaded every time you plug in
items.explanation: Flash is non-volatile: it retains its contents with the power off, so the program stored there is ready the moment you power on. RAM loses its contents at power-off, and the stack is just a region of RAM.

items.prompt: What is the first thing you should do when you suspect your program is too big to fit?
choices: Guess which feature is biggest and delete it
Check the size report to measure how much flash and RAM you actually use
Buy a bigger laptop
Add more comments to the code
items.explanation: Measure before you cut. The build tools print a size report showing your real flash and RAM usage, so you can target the biggest consumer instead of guessing. Guessing wastes effort and can remove the wrong thing.

items.prompt: What is the 'heap'?
choices: A pile of unused chips
Memory you request while the program runs and give back later (grow-on-demand)
The fastest part of flash
Another name for the program's code
items.explanation: The heap is grow-on-demand memory: the program asks for a chunk while running and returns it later. This flexibility is handy on big computers but risky on a tiny chip. It is not flash, and it is not the code itself.

items.prompt: Why is using dynamic (grow-on-demand) memory discouraged on a tiny microcontroller?
choices: It is illegal in the C language
On a chip with so little RAM, requests can fail unpredictably after the device has run for a while, and that is hard to recover from in the field
It makes the program run faster, which wastes battery
It only works when a laptop is connected
items.explanation: With only kilobytes of RAM, repeatedly asking for and returning memory can leave the free space broken into unusable scraps, so a later request fails at an unpredictable moment in a deployed device — with no easy recovery. It is legal, but unwise on tiny systems; fixed buffers avoid the surprise.

items.prompt: What is a 'static buffer', and why is it preferred on a microcontroller?
choices: A buffer that never moves on the screen; it looks nicer
A fixed-size piece of storage decided ahead of time, so you always know exactly how much memory it costs
A buffer made of static electricity
Memory that grows automatically when you need more
items.explanation: A static buffer has a fixed size chosen before the program runs, so its memory cost is known and guaranteed available. That predictability is exactly what you want on a tiny chip, unlike grow-on-demand heap memory that can run out unexpectedly.

items.prompt: Your program is a little too big for flash. The size report shows one feature's code is by far the largest. What is the most sensible move?
choices: Trim or simplify that largest feature and then re-measure
Rename your variables to shorter names
Delete random small functions until it fits
Move the program into RAM instead
items.explanation: Attack the biggest consumer first — that yields the most savings — then re-measure to confirm. Shorter variable names do not change compiled size, deleting at random is unreliable, and the program must live in flash.

items.prompt: Which statement about flash versus RAM is correct?
choices: Both are wiped at power-off
Flash keeps its contents at power-off; RAM is wiped at power-off
RAM keeps its contents at power-off; flash is wiped
Neither can store anything
items.explanation: Flash is non-volatile (keeps data with power off) and holds your program; RAM is volatile (wiped at power-off) and holds working data while running. That difference is why code lives in flash and scratch data lives in RAM.

lessons.title: What Is a Bootloader? The Program That Runs First
lessons.content:
**Hook.** When you switch on a device, *something* has to run before your main program can. On a microcontroller, that something is often a **bootloader** — a small program that runs first, does a little setup, and then hands control to your real program. Think of a hotel front desk: it greets you first, checks a few things, and then sends you to your room.

**Build.** Your real program is called the **firmware** — simply the software inside the device. ("Firm" because it usually lives in flash and changes rarely, unlike apps on a phone.) The bootloader is a separate, smaller program that lives in its own part of flash, near the start.

Why have a bootloader at all? Because it can do one very valuable job: **load a new version of the firmware.** It can receive a fresh copy of the main program, write it into flash, and then start it. That is how a device can be *updated in the field* — out in the real world, in someone's home — without any special equipment. Your Wi-Fi router, your phone, even a smart bulb gets software updates this way.

So the order of events at power-on is a simple chain:

1. **Power on / reset.** The chip wakes up.
2. **Bootloader runs first.** It may check for a new firmware to install, or just decide everything is fine.
3. **It hands control to the application** — your real firmware — which then runs normally.

The bootloader itself usually stays put and is not changed during an update; only the application part gets replaced. That separation is deliberate: the part that does the updating should not be able to break itself.

**Recap.** A bootloader is a small program that runs first when the chip powers on. Its key power is the ability to receive and install a new version of the firmware (the device's software), letting devices update themselves in the field, then it hands off to the application.

items.prompt: Use the boot-flow diagram. What is the correct order of events after power-on?
choices: Application runs first, then calls the bootloader
Power on → bootloader runs first → it hands control to the application
Power on → application → RAM → flash
The bootloader and application run at the same time, competing
items.explanation: At reset the bootloader runs first (like a front desk greeting you), does a little setup or update check, then hands control to the application. The application does not run before the bootloader, and they do not run simultaneously.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is a bootloader?
choices: A program that runs first when the chip powers on and can hand control to the main program
The physical boot-shaped connector on the board
A tool that runs only on your laptop to compile code
The largest program on the device
items.explanation: A bootloader is a small program that runs first at power-on, does setup (and optionally installs a new firmware), then hands control to the application. It is not a connector, not a laptop-only tool, and it is usually smaller than the application.

items.prompt: What does the word 'firmware' mean?
choices: The metal case of the device
The software inside the device (its program)
A firm handshake protocol
The hardest part of the silicon chip
items.explanation: Firmware is the software running inside an embedded device — its program. It is called 'firm' because it lives in flash and changes rarely compared to phone apps. It is not the case or the silicon itself.

items.prompt: What valuable job lets a bootloader update a device out in the field?
choices: It can speed up the CPU clock
It can receive a new firmware, write it into flash, and then start it
It can add more RAM to the chip
It can charge the battery faster
items.explanation: The bootloader's update superpower is receiving a fresh copy of the program, writing it into flash, and launching it — no special hardware or trip to a repair shop needed. It cannot add RAM, change battery hardware, or magically overclock the chip.

items.prompt: Which everyday example is firmware updating in the field, the way a bootloader enables?
choices: Your Wi-Fi router or smart bulb receiving a software update
Plugging in a new keyboard
Charging your phone overnight
Turning the volume up on a speaker
items.explanation: A router, phone, or smart bulb installing new software while sitting in your home is exactly field firmware update, made possible by a bootloader. Charging, plugging in peripherals, and adjusting volume do not change the device's software.

items.prompt: During a firmware update, which part of flash usually stays unchanged?
choices: The application
The bootloader
Both are erased every time
Neither part exists after an update
items.explanation: The bootloader normally stays put; only the application is replaced. Keeping the updater itself unchanged is deliberate — the part responsible for installing updates should not be able to overwrite and break itself.

items.prompt: Why is it useful that the bootloader and the application are separate programs in separate parts of flash?
choices: It makes the device heavier
So the part that installs updates is not replaced by the update and cannot break itself
Because two programs always run faster than one
It is required to charge the battery
items.explanation: Separation protects the updater: by leaving the bootloader untouched while only the application changes, you keep a reliable program that can always perform (or retry) updates. It has nothing to do with weight, speed, or charging.

items.prompt: The hotel front-desk analogy maps onto the boot flow how?
choices: The front desk is the application; your room is the bootloader
The front desk greets you first and then sends you to your room — like the bootloader running first and then handing off to the application
There is no order; you go straight to your room
The front desk and the room are the same place
items.explanation: The front desk (bootloader) handles you first, checks things, then directs you to your room (the application). That mirrors the bootloader running first at power-on and then handing control to the real program.

items.prompt: Compared to the application, the bootloader is usually:
choices: Much larger and more complex
Smaller, and stored near the start of flash
Stored only in RAM
Identical in size and purpose
items.explanation: The bootloader is typically a small program placed near the start of flash so it runs first. The application is the larger, full-featured program. The bootloader lives in flash (so it survives power-off), not only in RAM.

lessons.title: How Flash Works & How the Bootloader Jumps to the App
lessons.content:
**Hook.** To update firmware safely you first need to know one quirk of flash memory: you cannot just overwrite it like a notepad. And once a new program is in place, the bootloader has to *jump* to it correctly — do this wrong and the device won't start.

**Build.** *Flash is erase-before-write.* Writing to flash can only change bits in one direction. To truly rewrite a spot, you must first **erase** it back to blank. And erase does not work byte-by-byte — it works on a whole **sector** (a block, often several kilobytes) at a time. So to change a few bytes, you erase the entire block they live in, then write. There is also a wear limit: each sector can only be erased a finite number of times (often tens of thousands), so you avoid hammering one spot over and over — spreading writes out is called **wear leveling**.

Now the **flash layout**. Flash is divided into regions: the **bootloader** sits at the start, and the **application** sits in its own region at a higher address. The diagram shows this split (plus a spare slot we'll use later).

*The jump.* The application is built to live at its own address and starts with two key values at its very beginning: where its **stack** should start, and the address of its **first instruction** (its entry point). To launch the app, the bootloader, in order: (1) points the chip's *vector table* setting at the app's region, so interrupts go to the app's handlers; (2) sets the **stack pointer (SP)** to the app's stack start; (3) sets the **program counter (PC)** — the "what to run next" pointer — to the app's entry point. Setting the PC is the actual jump. Skipping the vector-table step is a classic bug: the app runs until the first interrupt, then crashes.

**Recap.** Flash must be erased (a whole sector at a time) before rewriting, and it wears out, so spread writes. The bootloader and app live in separate flash regions, and the bootloader launches the app by pointing the vector table, setting the stack pointer, and finally setting the program counter to the app's entry.

items.prompt: Use the flash-layout diagram. Which describes it correctly?
choices: The bootloader sits at the start; the application has its own region at a higher address, with a spare staging slot
There is only one region, shared by everything
The application sits before the bootloader
Flash holds only working variables, no code
items.explanation: The layout shows the immutable bootloader at the low address, the active application in its own region above it, and a spare slot for staging a new image. Flash is divided into regions and holds code, not just variables; the bootloader comes first.

items.prompt: What does 'erase-before-write' mean for flash?
choices: You can freely overwrite any byte at any time
To truly rewrite a location you must first erase it back to blank, and erase works on a whole block
Flash must be erased every single time you read it
Erasing and writing are the same operation
items.explanation: Flash writes can only change bits one way, so to genuinely change data you must erase first — and erase happens on a whole sector/block, not per byte. Reading never requires an erase, and erase and write are distinct steps.

items.prompt: You need to change just a few bytes that sit inside one flash sector. What must you do?
choices: Overwrite those few bytes directly
Erase only those few bytes
Erase the whole sector that contains them, then write the data back
Move the bytes into RAM permanently
items.explanation: Erase granularity is a whole sector, and you cannot rewrite bytes in place, so you erase the entire containing sector (saving any other data it held) and then program. You cannot erase just a few bytes, and the data must end up back in flash.

items.prompt: Why do designers avoid erasing the same flash sector over and over, and what is the fix called?
choices: It uses too much electricity; the fix is 'sleep mode'
Each sector can only be erased a limited number of times before wearing out; spreading writes around is called 'wear leveling'
It is too slow; the fix is 'overclocking'
Flash never wears out, so there is no concern
items.explanation: Flash sectors have a finite erase-cycle limit (often tens of thousands), so hammering one sector wears it out. Spreading writes across many locations — wear leveling — extends the flash's life. It is about endurance, not speed or power.

items.prompt: Where do the bootloader and the application live in flash?
choices: In the same bytes, taking turns
The bootloader near the start; the application in its own separate region at a higher address
Both only in RAM
The application near the start; the bootloader is not stored anywhere
items.explanation: Flash is split into regions: the bootloader sits near the start so it runs first, and the application occupies its own region higher up. They do not share the same bytes, and both are stored in flash, not RAM.

items.prompt: What two key values sit at the very beginning of the application, which the bootloader needs to launch it?
choices: The app's name and its version number
Where the stack should start, and the address of the app's first instruction (entry point)
The Wi-Fi password and the device serial number
The amount of free RAM and free flash
items.explanation: The application begins with its initial stack-pointer value and the address of its entry point (first instruction). The bootloader reads these to set the stack pointer and to jump. Names, versions, passwords, and free-space figures are not what the launch sequence needs.

items.prompt: What is the 'program counter' (PC), and how is it used in the jump to the app?
choices: A counter of how many programs are installed; it is reset to zero
The pointer to 'what instruction to run next'; setting it to the app's entry point is the actual jump
A timer that counts seconds since boot
The number of bytes the program uses
items.explanation: The program counter holds the address of the next instruction to execute. When the bootloader sets the PC to the application's entry point, the CPU starts running the app — that is the jump. It is not a count of programs, a clock, or a size.

items.prompt: A bootloader jumps to the app but forgets to point the vector table at the app's region. The app runs fine until its first interrupt, then crashes. Why?
choices: The stack pointer was never set, which only matters during interrupts
Interrupts are still routed to the bootloader's handler list instead of the app's, so the wrong code runs
Forgetting the vector table erases the application
Interrupts cannot happen unless the vector table is wrong
items.explanation: The vector table tells the chip where to find each interrupt's handler. If it still points at the bootloader's table, the first interrupt jumps into bootloader/garbage code relative to the running app, and it crashes. The crash appears only when an interrupt finally fires.

items.prompt: Put the bootloader's launch steps in the right order.
choices: Set the program counter first, then the stack pointer, then point the vector table
Point the vector table to the app, set the stack pointer, then set the program counter to the entry point
Erase the app, then set the stack pointer, then read its name
Only set the program counter; nothing else is needed
items.explanation: The safe order is: point the vector table at the app (so interrupts dispatch correctly), set the stack pointer to the app's stack start, then set the program counter to the entry point — that last step is the jump. Skipping the vector-table or stack-pointer step causes crashes.

lessons.title: Receiving & Writing a New Firmware Image
lessons.content:
**Hook.** So far the bootloader can run first and launch the app. Now for the interesting part: actually *getting* a new program onto the device and writing it into flash. This is the heart of a field update.

**Build.** The new program is packaged as an **image** — just a file containing the bytes of the new firmware (often a `.bin` file). The device receives this image over some **transport**, a link that carries the data:

- **UART** — a simple two-wire serial cable, common for wired updates on a workbench.
- **USB** — the same connector you use for many gadgets; a common style is called *DFU* (Device Firmware Upgrade) mode.
- **OTA** ("over the air") — *wireless*, such as Wi-Fi or Bluetooth. This is how your phone or smart bulb updates with no cable at all.

No matter the transport, the smart place to put the incoming image is **not** on top of the program that is currently running. Recall the flash layout had a **spare slot**. The device keeps running the current app in its slot while it writes the brand-new image into the *spare* slot. This is the safe choice: if the download is interrupted, the working app is still completely intact.

Writing the image obeys the flash rules from the previous lesson: **erase the spare slot first** (a sector at a time), then **program** the new bytes in. Because the image usually arrives in pieces, the device writes it chunk by chunk as the data streams in. Often the device also runs the flash-writing code from RAM, because on many chips the CPU cannot fetch instructions from a flash region while that same region is being erased or written.

**Recap.** A firmware image (the new program's bytes) arrives over a transport — UART, USB, or wireless OTA — and the device writes it into a *spare* slot (erase then program, chunk by chunk) while the current app keeps running, so an interrupted download never harms the working firmware.

items.prompt: Use the diagram of receiving an image. Where does the new image get written, and what is the running app doing meanwhile?
choices: Written over the running app, which must stop
Written into the spare slot (Slot B) while the current app in Slot A keeps running
Written into RAM permanently, never flash
Written into the bootloader region
items.explanation: The incoming image goes into the spare slot while the current app keeps running from its own slot. That way an interrupted download leaves the working app untouched. It does not overwrite the running app or the bootloader, and firmware lives in flash, not permanently in RAM.

items.prompt: What is a firmware 'image'?
choices: A photo of the circuit board
A file containing the bytes of the new firmware program
The icon shown on the device's screen
A picture used to test the camera
items.explanation: In firmware updates, an 'image' is the packaged bytes of the new program (often a .bin file) that will be written into flash. It has nothing to do with pictures or icons.

items.prompt: What does 'transport' mean in the context of firmware update?
choices: A truck that ships the device
The link that carries the image data to the device, such as UART, USB, or wireless
The part of flash that holds the image
Moving the device to a repair shop
items.explanation: The transport is the communication link that delivers the new image — a UART cable, a USB connection, or a wireless OTA link. It is not a vehicle and not a flash region.

items.prompt: What does OTA ('over the air') update mean?
choices: Updating using a wireless link such as Wi-Fi or Bluetooth, with no cable
Updating only while the device is airborne
A type of antenna
Updating by physically swapping the flash chip
items.explanation: OTA means the image arrives wirelessly (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, etc.) with no cable — how phones and smart bulbs update at home. It is not about flying or swapping chips, and it is a delivery method, not an antenna.

items.prompt: Why write the incoming image into a spare slot instead of over the currently running app?
choices: The spare slot is faster to write
So the working app stays intact — if the download is interrupted, the device still has a complete, runnable program
Because the running app's slot cannot be erased
It saves battery
items.explanation: Staging in the spare slot keeps the current app whole and bootable throughout the download. If the transfer fails partway, you have lost nothing — the old app still works. It is about safety, not speed or battery.

items.prompt: Writing the image into the spare slot follows which flash rule?
choices: Write directly; flash needs no preparation
Erase the slot first (a sector at a time), then program the new bytes
Program first, then erase
Only RAM rules apply, never flash rules
items.explanation: Flash is erase-before-write, so the spare slot is erased (sector by sector) and then the new bytes are programmed in. You cannot write without erasing first, and programming before erasing makes no sense.

items.prompt: The image often arrives in pieces. How does the device handle that?
choices: It refuses anything that is not delivered all at once
It writes the image chunk by chunk into the spare slot as the data streams in
It stores every chunk in the bootloader region
It can only accept images smaller than one byte
items.explanation: Because data streams in over the transport, the device programs it into the spare slot piece by piece as chunks arrive. It does not require the whole file at once, and chunks go into the staging slot, not the bootloader.

items.prompt: Why do devices often run the flash-writing code from RAM during an update?
choices: RAM code looks nicer
On many chips the CPU cannot fetch instructions from a flash region while that same region is being erased or written
RAM is the only place code can ever run
It permanently moves the program out of flash
items.explanation: Many chips cannot execute code out of a flash region while that region is mid-erase or mid-write. Running the writer from RAM avoids that conflict so the update can proceed. Code normally runs from flash; this is a special case during writing.

items.prompt: Which is a wired transport, and which is wireless, for delivering an image?
choices: UART and USB are wireless; OTA is wired
UART and USB are wired; OTA is wireless
All three are wireless
All three are wired
items.explanation: UART (a serial cable) and USB are wired transports; OTA is the wireless one (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth). Matching the transport to the situation — cable on a bench, OTA in the field — is part of designing an update path.

lessons.title: Safe Updates: Validate, A/B Swap & Rollback (Don't Brick It)
lessons.content:
**Hook.** The scary part of any update is failure. If the power is cut halfway through, or the new program is broken, the device could end up dead — unable to start at all. That dead state has a name: **bricked** (as useful as a brick). A well-designed update makes bricking nearly impossible. Here's how.

**Build.** Three ideas work together.

*1. Validate before trusting.* Once the new image is in the spare slot, the device checks it before ever running it. A **checksum** (a CRC) is a quick math fingerprint over the bytes: if even one byte got corrupted in transit, the fingerprint won't match, and the image is rejected. A stronger check is a **signature** — a cryptographic stamp proving the image really came from the legitimate maker, not an attacker. Rejecting on any mismatch means you never launch a bad image.

*2. A/B (two-slot) update with rollback.* Keep **two slots**: the running app stays in slot A while the new image goes into slot B (as in the last lesson). After validation, the bootloader switches over to slot B on the next restart. Crucially, the new image starts as "on trial": it must run a quick self-test and **confirm** it works. If it doesn't confirm — say it crashes, or a *watchdog* timer (a safety timer that resets the chip if the program stops responding) fires — the bootloader **rolls back**, returning to the known-good slot A. The diagram shows this whole flow.

Why does this beat bricking on power loss? Because the old app is *never erased while the new one is unproven.* Lose power mid-download, or get a bad image, and slot A is still there to boot. The switch only sticks once the new image proves itself.

*3. Secure boot, in one line:* the bootloader refuses to run any image whose signature isn't valid — so only trusted firmware ever runs.

You rarely build all this by hand. Ready-made systems exist — for example **MCUboot** (a popular open-source bootloader) and chip vendors' built-in update tools — that already implement validate, A/B swap, and rollback.

**Recap.** Avoid bricking by validating the image (checksum for corruption, signature for authenticity), using two slots so the working app is never erased until the new one is proven, and rolling back to the old slot if the new image fails to confirm. Secure boot means only validly signed images run; mature tools like MCUboot provide this off the shelf.

items.prompt: Use the update-and-rollback flow diagram. What happens after the new image is received into Slot B?
choices: It runs immediately with no checks
It is validated; if invalid it is rejected and the device keeps running A; if valid it is booted on trial and rolled back if it fails to confirm
Both slots are erased
The bootloader is replaced
items.explanation: The flow validates the image first; an invalid image is rejected (keep running A, no brick), and a valid one is booted on trial — confirmed if healthy, or rolled back to A by a watchdog/boot counter if it fails. It never runs unchecked, and the bootloader stays put.

items.prompt: What does it mean to 'brick' a device?
choices: To make it run faster
To leave it dead and unable to start at all, as useful as a brick
To add a brick-red case
To double its memory
items.explanation: Bricking means a failed update (or other mishap) leaves the device unable to boot — useless, like a brick. A safe update process is designed specifically to prevent this.

items.prompt: What does a checksum (CRC) tell you about a received image?
choices: Whether the bytes arrived uncorrupted (a fingerprint of the data)
Who the legal owner of the device is
How fast the image will run
The image's color scheme
items.explanation: A CRC is a quick math fingerprint over the bytes. If the image got corrupted in transit, the recomputed fingerprint won't match and the image is rejected. It checks for corruption, not ownership, speed, or appearance.

items.prompt: What does a cryptographic signature add beyond a checksum?
choices: It makes the file smaller
It proves the image genuinely came from the legitimate maker, not an attacker
It speeds up the download
Nothing; it is the same as a checksum
items.explanation: A checksum only catches accidental corruption — anyone could recompute it for a tampered image. A signature is a cryptographic stamp proving authenticity (it came from the trusted signer), which a checksum cannot do.

items.prompt: How does keeping two slots (A/B) plus validation prevent bricking when power is lost mid-update?
choices: It makes the download faster so power loss is less likely
The running app in slot A is never erased while the new image is unproven, so if power is lost mid-download slot A still boots
It stores the image twice in the same slot
It charges the battery during the update
items.explanation: The new image is staged and validated in slot B while slot A keeps running and is never erased until the new image proves itself. So a power cut mid-download leaves slot A intact and bootable — the device simply restarts the old firmware. The switch only sticks after validation and confirmation.

items.prompt: A new image is validated and booted, but it starts as 'on trial'. What must it do, and what happens if it doesn't?
choices: It must confirm it works; if it fails to confirm, the bootloader rolls back to the old slot
It must delete the old slot immediately
Nothing; trial mode lasts forever
It must increase its own version number
items.explanation: The new image runs a self-test and must explicitly confirm it is healthy. If it crashes or never confirms (e.g. a watchdog fires), the bootloader rolls back to the known-good previous slot on the next restart — the anti-brick safety net.

items.prompt: What is a watchdog timer's role in a safe update?
choices: It guards the building where the server lives
It is a safety timer that resets the chip if the program stops responding, which can trigger a rollback if the new image hangs
It counts how many images have been installed
It speeds up the CPU during updates
items.explanation: A watchdog resets the chip if the running program stops 'petting' it, meaning the program has hung. If a freshly installed image hangs before confirming, the watchdog reset lets the bootloader roll back to the known-good slot. It is a safety mechanism, not a counter or speed booster.

items.prompt: An image passes its checksum but its signature does not verify. What is the correct action?
choices: Run it anyway, since the checksum proves it is fine
Reject it and keep running the current app; a valid checksum does not prove the image is from a trusted source
Run it but turn off the watchdog
Ask the user to retype the signature
items.explanation: A good checksum only means the bytes are uncorrupted, not that they are authentic. A failed signature means the image is unauthenticated (possibly tampered with), so secure boot must reject it and keep the trusted app running.

items.prompt: What is 'secure boot', in one line?
choices: Booting only after the user logs in
The bootloader refuses to run any image whose signature is not valid, so only trusted firmware runs
Booting as fast as possible
Booting only when plugged into a charger
items.explanation: Secure boot means the bootloader checks each image's signature and runs it only if it is validly signed by the trusted maker — so only trusted firmware ever executes. It is about authenticity, not login, speed, or charging.

items.prompt: Why might you use a ready-made system like MCUboot instead of writing all the update logic yourself?
choices: It is required by law
It already implements validation, A/B swap, and rollback correctly, so you avoid reinventing tricky, safety-critical logic
It makes the device physically larger
It is the only way to turn on an LED
items.explanation: MCUboot is a popular open-source bootloader that already provides signed-image validation, A/B swap, and rollback — logic that is subtle and easy to get dangerously wrong. Using it (or a vendor's built-in tool) saves effort and reduces bricking risk. It is a choice, not a legal requirement.
